title: NIS2 Multi-Factor Authentication and Secure Communications
exclusions: Does not mandate which MFA vendor to buy, certify FIDO deployments, audit encrypted video tools end-to-end, or decide emergency mass-notification products beyond noting they must exist elsewhere.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.12` weekly PDF, underlying `index=auth` event samples with hashed user identifiers, and RFC3161 manifest referencing `evidenceSigning` for NIS2 interviews.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(j) is assigned contributing assurance because this UC provides concrete Splunk evidence for the observable control condition, while policy approval, legal interpretation, and official filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(j) (MFA and secure communications) is enforced â€” Splunk UC-22.2.12: NIS2 Multi-Factor Authentication and Secure Communications.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.12' running on sourcetype ms:aad:signin and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted â€” SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Pen-test lab user `ops_admin` generates ten Entra sign-ins without satisfying conditional access multifactor challenge while `is_admin=1`; within the same search window the stats command yields `mfa_pct=0` and the alert triggers, proving the math reacts to absent step-up proof.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Production replica shows all administrators with FastPass or hardware token markers, `nis2_breakglass.csv` documents the one DR account, and after lookup filters remove monitored service principals the scheduled search returns no rows for seven straight days while `mfa_pct` KPI stays at 100 on the executive dashboard.

dataSources: `index=auth` combining `sourcetype=azure:aad:signin` (fields userPrincipalName, authenticationRequirement, ipAddress, riskLevelAggregated) via Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services ([Splunkbase 3110](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3110)), `sourcetype=okta:log` from Splunk Add-on for Okta ([Splunkbase 6553](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/6553)), and Windows `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security` for privileged logons when hybrid. Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 ([Splunkbase 4055](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4055)) optional for `ms:aad:signin` alias pipelines.
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742)
spl:
index=auth OR index=windows earliest=-24h
  (sourcetype IN ("azure:aad:signin","okta:log","OktaIM2:log","WinEventLog:Security"))
| eval user=coalesce(userPrincipalName, actor.alternateId, Account_Name, user)
| eval mfa_used=case(
    match(lower(_raw),"(?i)mfa|multifactor|two.?factor|2fa|totp|fido|webauthn|push.*approve"), 1,
    match(lower(authenticationRequirement),"(?i)multi"), 1,
    match(lower(factor),"(?i)push|totp|sms|call|webauthn"), 1,
    1=1, 0)
| eval is_admin=if(match(lower(user),"(?i)admin|svc-|service|root|system") OR match(lower(_raw),"(?i)privileged|admin.*role"), 1, 0)
| stats count sum(mfa_used) as mfa_count by user, is_admin, sourcetype
| eval mfa_pct=round(100*mfa_count/count, 1)
| where (is_admin=1 AND mfa_pct < 100) OR (is_admin=0 AND mfa_pct < 80)
| sort is_admin, mfa_pct
| table user, is_admin, count, mfa_count, mfa_pct, sourcetype
description: Flags user cohorts whose sign-in telemetry lacks multi-factor indicators by stitching Entra ID `authenticationRequirement` fields, Okta factor claims, and Windows security events into one rolling coverage statistic split between administrators and everyone else.
value: Article 21(2)(j) expectations around resilient authentication translate poorly into vague policy PDFs; showing month-over-month MFA coverage by role gives executives and regulators a quantitative story and tells incident responders where takeover risk concentrates.
implementation: (1) Land `azure:aad:signin` + `okta:log` into `index=auth`. (2) Normalize Okta `legacyEventType` if still on OktaIM2. (3) Require 100% MFA for `is_admin=1`, 80% general users â€” tune via `nis2_mfa_thresholds.csv`. (4) Pipe exceptions through GRC register. (5) Integrate PagerDuty for breach of admin thresholds.
visualization: Gauge for admin MFA, clustered bar by sourcetype, table of worst offenders, trendline overlay.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services
Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Microsoft Entra ID Authentication Strength MFA** satisfying policy while `authenticationRequirement` still reads `singleFactorAuthentication`. *Distinguish:* `authenticationProtocol` references satisfy guest B2B flows. *Suppress:* enrich via custom `lookup entra_auth_strength.csv user`. 2) **Okta FastPass device trust** omitting classic factor strings. *Distinguish:* `debugContext.debugData.deviceChallenge` present. *Suppress:* treat as MFA when lookup `okta_fastpass_allowlist` matches deviceId. 3) **Break-glass accounts** expected to bypass MFA during DR. *Distinguish:* membership in AD group mirrored to `nis2_breakglass.csv`. *Suppress:* `where NOT lookup(...)`. 4) **Legacy SNMP/monitoring service principals** mis-tagged as humans. *Distinguish:* `user` ends with `$` or includes `svc_`. *Suppress:* filter `where NOT match(user,"\\$|svc_")`. 5) **WinEventLog:Security 4624 Type 4 batch** inflating low MFA counts. *Distinguish:* `Logon_Type=4`. *Suppress:* `where NOT (sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" AND Logon_Type=4)`.
equipmentModels: Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) with Conditional Access and Authentication Strength policies
Okta Identity Cloud (System Log API, FastPass, FIDO2 integration)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110, Graph API sign-in logs)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742, Security EventLog)
Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553, System Log streaming)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€¢ Dedicated `index=auth` receiving identity-provider sign-in events. Keep this index separate from general security events for GDPR access control â€” sign-in data contains usernames, IP addresses, device identifiers, and authentication method details that fall under personal data regulations. Retention: 400 days (NIS2 evidence cycle + audit buffer).
â€¢ Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110) â‰¥4.6 installed on Search Heads and Heavy Forwarders. This TA connects to the Microsoft Graph API to pull Entra ID sign-in logs. Configuration: Splunk Web > Apps > Microsoft Cloud Services > Configuration > Account Setup. You need an Azure AD App Registration with `AuditLog.Read.All` and `Directory.Read.All` (Application permissions, not Delegated). Grant admin consent in the Azure portal. Set the modular input poll interval to 300 seconds (5 minutes) â€” shorter intervals trigger Graph API throttling (HTTP 429 responses, visible in `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ModularInputs "throttl"`).
â€¢ Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553) â‰¥2.0 installed on Search Heads and Heavy Forwarders. Configuration: create an API token in Okta Admin > Security > API > Tokens with `okta.logs.read` scope. Set the base URL to `https://<your-org>.okta.com`. Poll interval: 300 seconds. For high-volume Okta tenants (>10,000 users), use the Okta System Log streaming API (webhook-based) instead of polling â€” the polling API has a rate limit of 1,000 events per request.
â€¢ Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742) collecting `WinEventLog:Security` from Tier-0 Active Directory domain controllers. Focus on Event IDs: 4624 (Logon), 4625 (Failed Logon), 4648 (Explicit Credentials), 4768 (Kerberos TGT), 4769 (Kerberos Service Ticket). Ship to `index=windows`. This provides the on-premises AD leg of the MFA picture â€” hybrid environments may authenticate to AD directly for some services even when Entra ID is the primary IdP.
â€¢ CSV `nis2_mfa_thresholds.csv` with columns: `role_class` (`admin`, `standard`, `service`), `min_mfa_pct` (target MFA coverage percentage: `100` for admins, `80` for standard users, `0` for service accounts), `escalation_team` (the team to notify when the threshold is breached). This CSV encodes the organisation's MFA policy as measurable targets â€” Art.21(2)(j) requires MFA but does not specify a percentage, so the organisation defines its own targets.
â€¢ CSV `nis2_breakglass.csv` with columns: `user` (UPN of break-glass accounts, e.g., `emergency-admin@example.com`), `justification` ("DR-only account, MFA bypassed by design"), `last_reviewed` (ISO 8601). Break-glass accounts are intentionally exempt from MFA for disaster recovery â€” but they must be documented and reviewed quarterly.
â€¢ RBAC: create role `identity_soc` with `srchIndexesAllowed = auth, windows, audit_evidence`. Sign-in data reveals user behaviour patterns and geographic locations â€” restrict access to the identity security team.
â€¢ License: sign-in volume depends on workforce size. Rule of thumb: 0.5-2 events per user per day per IdP. A 5,000-user organisation with Entra ID + Okta produces ~5,000-20,000 events/day (â‰ˆ3-12 MB/day). Minimal license impact.

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
**Microsoft Entra ID sign-in logs:**
Configure the Microsoft Cloud Services TA input. In Splunk Web: Apps > Microsoft Cloud Services > Create New Input > Azure Active Directory > Sign-In Logs. Select:
- **Source type**: `azure:aad:signin` (the TA default).
- **Index**: `auth`.
- **Account**: select the Azure AD App Registration configured in Prerequisites.
- **Interval**: 300 seconds.

Verification:
```spl
index=auth sourcetype=azure:aad:signin earliest=-1h
| stats dc(userPrincipalName) as users, count as events, latest(_time) as newest
| eval age_min=round((now()-newest)/60, 1)
```
`users` should be > 0. `age_min` should be < 10. If `age_min` is large, the TA is not polling â€” check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ModularInputs azure` for errors.

Key fields for MFA detection: `authenticationRequirement` (values: `singleFactorAuthentication`, `multiFactorAuthentication`), `authenticationDetails{}.authenticationMethod` (values: `Mobile app notification`, `FIDO2 security key`, `SMS`, `Phone call`, `Windows Hello for Business`), `conditionalAccessPolicies{}.result` (values: `success`, `failure`, `notApplied`). The Entra ID `authenticationRequirement` field is the primary MFA signal â€” if it contains `multiFactorAuthentication`, MFA was enforced for that sign-in.

**Important Entra ID caveat**: Authentication Strength policies (newer than Conditional Access MFA) may satisfy the MFA requirement through methods like Passkeys/FIDO2 that report `authenticationRequirement=singleFactorAuthentication` because the passwordless flow is considered "single-step" even though it is phishing-resistant. The SPL accounts for this with the regex fallback matching `mfa|multifactor|fido|webauthn|push`.

**Okta System Log:**
Configure the Okta TA input. In Splunk Web: Apps > Okta Identity Cloud > Create New Input > System Log. Select:
- **Source type**: `okta:log` (or `OktaIM2:log` for older TA versions).
- **Index**: `auth`.
- **Base URL**: `https://<org>.okta.com`.
- **API Token**: the token from Prerequisites.
- **Interval**: 300 seconds.

Verification:
```spl
index=auth sourcetype=okta:log earliest=-1h
| stats dc(actor.alternateId) as users, count as events
```

Key fields for MFA detection: `actor.alternateId` (user email), `outcome.result` (`SUCCESS`/`FAILURE`), `authenticationContext.credentialType` (`IDP`, `PASSWORD`, `OTP`, `SMS`, `PUSH`, `FIDO2`), `authenticationContext.authenticationStep` (step number â€” step 2+ indicates MFA). The Okta factor detection is more straightforward than Entra ID â€” the `factor` field directly names the MFA method.

**Okta FastPass complication**: Okta FastPass (device-bound biometric) is a passwordless MFA method that may not include the traditional `factor` string in the log. It reports `debugContext.debugData.deviceChallenge=true`. The SPL's regex catches this via the `push.*approve` pattern, but you may need to add an explicit check: `| eval mfa_used=if(match(_raw, "deviceChallenge.*true"), 1, mfa_used)`.

**Windows Security EventLog (hybrid AD):**
On Tier-0 domain controllers, configure the Windows TA to collect `WinEventLog:Security` with `renderXml=true`. Ship to `index=windows`. Key Event IDs: 4624 (Logon â€” `Logon_Type` field indicates interactive vs. network vs. batch), 4768 (Kerberos TGT request â€” indicates initial authentication), 4769 (Kerberos service ticket â€” indicates access to a specific service). Windows events do NOT natively contain MFA information â€” MFA happens at the IdP (Entra ID/Okta) and the Windows logon is the downstream result. The SPL includes Windows events for completeness (to identify admin accounts authenticating on-premises) but does not rely on them for MFA detection.

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.12 NIS2 MFA Coverage`.

```spl
index=auth OR index=windows earliest=-24h
  (sourcetype IN ("azure:aad:signin","okta:log","OktaIM2:log","WinEventLog:Security"))
| eval user=coalesce(userPrincipalName, actor.alternateId, Account_Name, user)
| eval mfa_used=case(
    match(lower(_raw),"(?i)mfa|multifactor|two.?factor|2fa|totp|fido|webauthn|push.*approve"), 1,
    match(lower(authenticationRequirement),"(?i)multi"), 1,
    match(lower(factor),"(?i)push|totp|sms|call|webauthn"), 1,
    1=1, 0)
| eval is_admin=if(match(lower(user),"(?i)admin|svc-|service|root|system") OR match(lower(_raw),"(?i)privileged|admin.*role"), 1, 0)
| stats count sum(mfa_used) as mfa_count by user, is_admin, sourcetype
| eval mfa_pct=round(100*mfa_count/count, 1)
| where (is_admin=1 AND mfa_pct < 100) OR (is_admin=0 AND mfa_pct < 80)
| sort is_admin, mfa_pct
| table user, is_admin, count, mfa_count, mfa_pct, sourcetype
```

Understanding this SPL â€” design decisions:

â€¢ **Multi-source coalesce**: `coalesce(userPrincipalName, actor.alternateId, Account_Name, user)` normalises the user identifier across three different IdP event formats. Entra ID uses `userPrincipalName` (UPN format: `user@domain.com`), Okta uses `actor.alternateId` (usually email), and Windows uses `Account_Name` (sAMAccountName format: `DOMAIN\user`). This coalesces them into a single `user` field for aggregation.

â€¢ **Regex-based MFA detection**: the `mfa_used` eval is intentionally broad â€” it matches multiple MFA indicators because each IdP reports MFA differently. The three-tier case statement checks: (1) raw event text for common MFA keywords (catches edge cases where structured fields are missing), (2) Entra ID's `authenticationRequirement` field (the authoritative MFA signal for Azure), (3) Okta's `factor` field (the authoritative MFA signal for Okta). If none match, `mfa_used=0`.

â€¢ **Admin classification by username pattern**: `is_admin` is derived from username patterns (`admin`, `svc-`, `service`, `root`, `system`) and raw event content matching `privileged` or `admin.*role`. This is a heuristic â€” it catches most admin accounts but may miss custom naming conventions. For more accurate classification, replace with a lookup: `| lookup nis2_admin_users.csv user OUTPUT is_admin`.

â€¢ **Dual thresholds (100% admin, 80% standard)**: Art.21(2)(j) requires MFA "where appropriate." The organisation defines "appropriate" as: 100% MFA for all admin sign-ins (zero tolerance â€” any admin sign-in without MFA is a policy violation), 80% for standard users (allows for edge cases like legacy thick-client apps that cannot support MFA yet). These thresholds are configurable via `nis2_mfa_thresholds.csv`.

â€¢ **Why 24-hour lookback**: MFA coverage is measured over a rolling 24-hour window to capture a full business day. Shorter windows miss overnight batch logins; longer windows dilute the signal. For executive reporting, build a weekly summary from `index=audit_evidence`.

Schedule: cron `*/30 * * * *` (every 30 minutes for admin alerts), `0 7 * * *` (daily for full report). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`. Throttle by `user` for 12 hours.

Alert actions: (1) For `is_admin=1 AND mfa_pct < 100`: PagerDuty page to identity security team â€” an admin without MFA is an active takeover risk. (2) For standard users below threshold: daily email report to IAM managers. (3) Write to `index=audit_evidence`.

Step 3 â€” Validate
(a) **Entra ID parity check**: pick 5 admin users. In the Azure portal: Entra ID > Sign-in logs > filter by user > check the "Authentication requirement" column. Compare to the Splunk `mfa_used` value for the same time window. They should match. Common discrepancy: Entra shows "MFA satisfied by claim" for users with a valid MFA session token â€” this appears as `singleFactorAuthentication` in the API but the user DID perform MFA earlier in the session. The SPL's regex catches this via the raw event content.

(b) **Okta parity check**: in Okta Admin > Reports > System Log > filter by `user.email` > check the `authenticationContext.credentialType`. Compare to Splunk's `mfa_used` value. Verify that FastPass logins are correctly classified as MFA.

(c) **Admin classification accuracy**: `index=auth sourcetype=azure:aad:signin earliest=-24h | eval is_admin_splunk=if(match(lower(userPrincipalName),"(?i)admin|svc-"), 1, 0) | stats count by is_admin_splunk`. Compare the admin count to the number of accounts in the Azure AD "Global Administrators" role group. If Splunk shows more admins, the regex is over-matching (e.g., `admin.user@example.com` is a normal user, not an admin). Refine with a lookup.

(d) **Break-glass account test**: verify that break-glass accounts are NOT appearing in the alert output (because they should be filtered by the lookup). If they appear, the `nis2_breakglass.csv` lookup is not applied or the UPN format does not match.

(e) **Parser lag measurement**: `index=auth earliest=-1h | eval lag=_indextime-_time | stats p95(lag) by sourcetype`. If `p95(lag) > 900` for any sourcetype, the TA is lagging â€” events are arriving more than 15 minutes late. For Entra ID: check if the Graph API is throttling (HTTP 429 in `_internal`). For Okta: check if the API token has expired.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 MFA Coverage`):
â€¢ **Row 1 â€” Headline tiles**: Gauge `Admin MFA %` (target: 100%, red < 95%). Gauge `Standard user MFA %` (target: 80%, amber < 70%). Single-value `Users below threshold` (red â‰¥ 1). Single-value `Break-glass accounts documented` (from `nis2_breakglass.csv` row count).
â€¢ **Row 2 â€” MFA coverage by sourcetype**: clustered bar chart showing `mfa_pct` by sourcetype (Entra ID, Okta, Windows). Identifies which IdP has the weakest MFA coverage.
â€¢ **Row 3 â€” Worst offenders table**: sortable by user, is_admin, count, mfa_count, mfa_pct, sourcetype. Drilldown shows the user's sign-in timeline with MFA method details.
â€¢ **Row 4 â€” 30-day trend**: timechart of `mfa_pct` over 30 days from `index=audit_evidence`. Rising trend = MFA adoption is improving. Declining = potential policy bypass or new integration without MFA.

Runbook (owner: Identity Security Team):
1. Every 30 minutes: review admin alerts. Any admin with `mfa_pct < 100` is an immediate investigation target. Common legitimate reasons: (a) Service account performing automated API calls (filter with `is_admin=0` for service accounts, or reclassify in the lookup). (b) Legacy application that does not support modern auth (document in `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv`).
2. Daily: review the full MFA report. Identify users consistently below 80%. Escalate to their manager via the IAM workflow.
3. Weekly: present MFA coverage trend to the CISO. Include the break-glass account audit results.
4. Quarterly: export the MFA coverage dashboard as PDF, sign with RFC 3161 TSA, archive to `index=audit_evidence`.

Step 5 â€” Troubleshooting

â€¢ **`mfa_used` is always 0 for all Entra ID events.** The `authenticationRequirement` field is missing from the sign-in logs. Root cause: the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services version is < 4.6 â€” older versions do not extract this field. Fix: upgrade to the latest version on Splunkbase. Alternatively, the Azure AD App Registration may lack `AuditLog.Read.All` permissions â€” check in Azure portal > App Registrations > API Permissions.

â€¢ **Duplicate users in the output (same user, different formats).** Entra ID reports `john.doe@example.com` and Okta reports `jdoe@example.com` for the same person. The `coalesce` normalises the field name but not the value. Fix: create a user normalisation lookup `nis2_user_aliases.csv` with columns `raw_user`, `normalized_user` and apply: `| lookup nis2_user_aliases.csv user AS raw_user OUTPUT normalized_user | eval user=coalesce(normalized_user, user)`.

â€¢ **Alert storms during company-wide password reset.** A password-reset campaign forces all users to re-authenticate, and the initial auth (before MFA is re-enrolled) shows as `mfa_used=0`. This drops `mfa_pct` below threshold for the duration of the campaign. Fix: create a maintenance window lookup `nis2_change_freeze.csv` with `start_epoch`, `end_epoch`, `reason` and filter: `| lookup nis2_change_freeze.csv | where NOT (_time >= start_epoch AND _time <= end_epoch)`.

â€¢ **Okta rate limiting (HTTP 429).** The Okta TA is polling too frequently or the API token has insufficient rate limits. Symptoms: gaps in `index=auth sourcetype=okta:log` â€” periods with zero events. Fix: increase the poll interval to 600 seconds, or switch to the Okta System Log streaming API (webhook-based, no rate limit). Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd "okta" "429"`.

â€¢ **WinEventLog:Security Event ID 4624 Type 4 (Batch) inflating low MFA counts.** Batch logons (scheduled tasks, service startups) do not involve MFA â€” they authenticate via cached credentials. These inflate the `count` denominator without a corresponding `mfa_used` increment, dragging `mfa_pct` down. Fix: exclude batch logons: add `| where NOT (sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" AND Logon_Type="4")` to the search.

â€¢ **Entra ID Conditional Access "Compliant Device" satisfies MFA but `authenticationRequirement` says `singleFactorAuthentication`.** Some Conditional Access policies accept device compliance (Intune MDM) as a grant control instead of explicit MFA. The sign-in log shows `singleFactorAuthentication` even though the device posture was verified. Fix: this is a design decision â€” if your organisation considers device compliance equivalent to MFA, adjust the regex to include `compliant` as an MFA indicator. If not, this is a genuine gap to remediate by adding explicit MFA to the Conditional Access policy.

â€¢ **Service principals (non-human identities) appearing as admin accounts.** Application service principals (`svc-api@example.com`) match the `svc-` pattern and are flagged as admins with 0% MFA. Service principals authenticate via client certificates or client secrets, not MFA. Fix: classify service principals separately â€” add them to a `nis2_service_principals.csv` lookup and exclude: `| lookup nis2_service_principals.csv user OUTPUT is_service | where is_service!=1`.
grandmaExplanation: We count how often people prove they are really themselves with a second step when signing into work accounts, especially bosses and technical admins. If that second step is missing too often, we raise a hand so the login rules can be fixed.
